title: Osimertinib in Untreated EGFR-Mutated Advanced Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA)
text_chunk: The FLAURA trial was a randomized, double-blind, phase 3 study comparing osimertinib, a third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor, with standard EGFR-TKIs (gefitinib or erlotinib) as first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC. A total of 556 patients with EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutations were enrolled. The primary endpoint was investigator-assessed progression-free survival. Osimertinib demonstrated a significantly longer median PFS of 18.9 months compared with 10.2 months for the comparator TKIs (hazard ratio 0.46, 95% CI 0.37-0.57, p<0.001). Notably, osimertinib showed superior CNS efficacy with a CNS PFS HR of 0.48. The overall survival analysis published subsequently showed a median OS of 38.6 months for osimertinib versus 31.8 months for comparator TKIs (HR 0.80, p=0.046). The safety profile of osimertinib was favorable, with lower rates of grade 3 or higher adverse events (34% vs 45%). This trial established osimertinib as the preferred first-line EGFR TKI and fundamentally changed the treatment paradigm for EGFR-mutant NSCLC by demonstrating that starting with a third-generation agent improves both PFS and OS compared with sequential therapy.

title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan versus Trastuzumab Emtansine for Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast03)
text_chunk: DESTINY-Breast03 was a pivotal phase 3 randomized trial comparing trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) with trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1) in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer previously treated with trastuzumab and a taxane. The trial enrolled 524 patients and demonstrated a dramatic improvement in progression-free survival with T-DXd. At the prespecified interim analysis, the 12-month PFS rate was 75.8% with T-DXd versus 34.1% with T-DM1 (HR 0.28, 95% CI 0.22-0.37, p<0.001). Median PFS was not reached for T-DXd versus 6.8 months for T-DM1. The objective response rate was 79.7% versus 34.2%, including complete responses in 16.1% versus 8.7%. Updated overall survival data showed a median OS of 52.6 months with T-DXd compared with estimated 42.7 months for T-DM1. Interstitial lung disease (ILD), a class effect of DXd-based ADCs, occurred in 15.2% of patients receiving T-DXd (mostly grade 1-2), with adjudicated drug-related deaths in 0.8%. This trial fundamentally changed the second-line treatment paradigm for HER2-positive breast cancer and established T-DXd as one of the most active agents in breast oncology.

title: Pembrolizumab versus Chemotherapy for PD-L1-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (KEYNOTE-024)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-024 was a landmark phase 3 randomized trial that established single-agent immunotherapy as first-line treatment for advanced NSCLC with high PD-L1 expression. The trial enrolled 305 patients with previously untreated stage IV NSCLC whose tumors expressed PD-L1 on at least 50% of tumor cells (TPS >= 50%) and had no sensitizing EGFR mutations or ALK fusions. Patients were randomized to pembrolizumab 200 mg every 3 weeks or investigator-choice platinum-doublet chemotherapy. Pembrolizumab significantly improved median PFS to 10.3 months versus 6.0 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.50, 95% CI 0.37-0.68, p<0.001). The overall response rate was 44.8% versus 27.8%. Updated long-term data showed a median OS of 26.3 months for pembrolizumab versus 14.2 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.62). Grade 3-5 treatment-related adverse events were less common with pembrolizumab (26.6% vs 53.3%). Immune-mediated adverse events included hypothyroidism (9.1%), pneumonitis (5.8%), and infusion reactions (10.4%). This study validated PD-L1 TPS as a predictive biomarker for immunotherapy benefit and established pembrolizumab monotherapy as the standard of care for PD-L1-high NSCLC.

title: Pembrolizumab in Microsatellite-Instability-High Advanced Colorectal Cancer (KEYNOTE-177)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-177 was a phase 3 randomized trial evaluating pembrolizumab versus standard chemotherapy as first-line treatment for microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) metastatic colorectal cancer. The trial enrolled 307 patients and randomized them to pembrolizumab 200 mg every 3 weeks or investigator-choice chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab or cetuximab. Pembrolizumab demonstrated a significantly longer median PFS of 16.5 months versus 8.2 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.60, 95% CI 0.45-0.80, p=0.0002). The objective response rate was 43.8% for pembrolizumab versus 33.1% for chemotherapy, with a notably higher complete response rate of 11.1% versus 3.9%. Responses with pembrolizumab were more durable with a median duration of response not reached versus 10.6 months. Grade 3-5 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 22% of pembrolizumab patients versus 66% of chemotherapy patients. This trial established immunotherapy as the new first-line standard for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC and reinforced MSI/MMR testing as essential for all patients with metastatic colorectal cancer. Crossover from chemotherapy to pembrolizumab was permitted and 36% of chemotherapy patients crossed over.

title: Olaparib Monotherapy in Patients with Advanced Cancer and a Germline BRCA Mutation (OlympiAD)
text_chunk: OlympiAD was a phase 3 randomized trial evaluating the PARP inhibitor olaparib versus physician's choice of single-agent chemotherapy (capecitabine, eribulin, or vinorelbine) in patients with HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer and a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation. A total of 302 patients who had received no more than two prior lines of chemotherapy for metastatic disease were enrolled. Olaparib demonstrated a significant improvement in median PFS of 7.0 months versus 4.2 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.58, 95% CI 0.43-0.80, p<0.001). The objective response rate was 59.9% with olaparib versus 28.8% with chemotherapy. Subgroup analysis showed a consistent PFS benefit across hormone receptor-positive and triple-negative breast cancer subtypes. Grade 3 or higher adverse events occurred in 36.6% with olaparib versus 50.5% with chemotherapy, with nausea and anemia being the most common side effects. Quality of life was significantly better with olaparib. Although the final OS analysis did not show a statistically significant difference (19.3 vs 17.1 months, HR 0.90), this was confounded by crossover. This trial led to FDA approval of olaparib for germline BRCA-mutated HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer and established PARP inhibitors as a therapeutic class in breast oncology.

title: Sotorasib for KRAS p.G12C-Mutated Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (CodeBreaK 100)
text_chunk: CodeBreaK 100 was a phase 2 single-arm study evaluating sotorasib, the first KRAS G12C-specific inhibitor, in patients with KRAS G12C-mutated advanced NSCLC who had received prior platinum-based chemotherapy and a PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor. A total of 126 patients were enrolled and received sotorasib 960 mg daily. The primary endpoint was objective response rate by blinded independent central review. Sotorasib achieved an ORR of 37.1% (95% CI 28.6-46.2), including 3.2% complete responses, with a disease control rate of 80.6%. The median duration of response was 11.1 months. Median PFS was 6.8 months and median OS was 12.5 months. Treatment-related adverse events of grade 3 or higher occurred in 19.8% of patients, with diarrhea (31.7%), nausea (19.0%), and elevated liver enzymes (15.1%) being the most common toxicities. The responses were observed across different PD-L1 expression levels and co-mutation subtypes including STK11 and KEAP1 co-mutations. This trial was historic as it demonstrated the clinical druggability of KRAS G12C, a target previously considered undruggable for decades, and led to accelerated FDA approval of sotorasib in May 2021.

title: Lorlatinib in Previously Untreated ALK-Positive Lung Cancer (CROWN)
text_chunk: The CROWN trial was a phase 3 randomized study comparing lorlatinib, a third-generation ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitor, with crizotinib as first-line treatment in patients with ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. A total of 296 patients were randomized 1:1 to lorlatinib 100 mg once daily or crizotinib 250 mg twice daily. At the initial analysis, lorlatinib demonstrated a significant improvement in PFS with a 12-month PFS rate of 78% versus 39% for crizotinib (HR 0.28, 95% CI 0.19-0.41, p<0.001). Updated 5-year follow-up data were remarkable: median PFS was 60 months for lorlatinib versus 9.3 months for crizotinib, with a 5-year PFS rate of 60% for lorlatinib. The intracranial response rate was 82% with lorlatinib versus 23% with crizotinib among patients with measurable brain metastases. The overall response rate was 77% for lorlatinib versus 58% for crizotinib. Common adverse events with lorlatinib included hypercholesterolemia (81%), hypertriglyceridemia (60%), edema (55%), and CNS effects including cognitive effects (21%). This trial established lorlatinib as a first-line option for ALK-positive NSCLC with unprecedented duration of disease control and CNS activity.

title: Combined Nivolumab and Ipilimumab versus Ipilimumab Alone in Previously Untreated Advanced Melanoma (CheckMate-067)
text_chunk: CheckMate-067 was a landmark phase 3 trial that established dual checkpoint inhibition as a standard of care for advanced melanoma. The trial randomized 945 previously untreated patients with unresectable stage III or IV melanoma to nivolumab plus ipilimumab, nivolumab alone, or ipilimumab alone. At the initial analysis, median PFS was 11.5 months for the combination, 6.9 months for nivolumab, and 2.9 months for ipilimumab. The landmark 6.5-year follow-up demonstrated remarkable durability: median OS was more than 72 months for nivolumab plus ipilimumab, 36.9 months for nivolumab, and 19.9 months for ipilimumab. The 6.5-year OS rates were 49%, 42%, and 23% respectively. BRAF V600E-mutant patients had numerically higher response rates with the combination. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 59% of combination patients, 23% of nivolumab patients, and 28% of ipilimumab patients. Despite the high toxicity rate, treatment-related deaths were rare (1.7% with combination) and most immune-related adverse events were manageable with established algorithms. This trial transformed the melanoma treatment landscape and demonstrated that durable long-term survival is achievable with immunotherapy.

title: Efficacy of Selpercatinib in RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (LIBRETTO-001)
text_chunk: LIBRETTO-001 was a phase 1/2 open-label study evaluating selpercatinib, a highly selective and potent RET kinase inhibitor, in patients with RET fusion-positive cancers including NSCLC and thyroid cancer. Among 105 previously treated RET fusion-positive NSCLC patients, selpercatinib achieved an objective response rate of 64% (95% CI 54-73) with a median duration of response of 17.5 months. In 39 treatment-naive RET fusion-positive NSCLC patients, the ORR was 85% (95% CI 70-94). Intracranial activity was notable, with an intracranial ORR of 91% among 11 patients with measurable CNS metastases. The median PFS was 16.5 months in previously treated patients. Grade 3-4 adverse events included hypertension (14%), elevated ALT (12%), elevated AST (10%), and hyponatremia (6%). Selpercatinib was effective regardless of the RET fusion partner, with KIF5B-RET and CCDC6-RET being the most common. This study demonstrated the clinical benefit of precision oncology in a rare molecular subset (1-2% of NSCLC) and led to the first FDA approval specifically for RET fusion-positive cancers in May 2020.

title: Sacituzumab Govitecan in Metastatic Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (ASCENT)
text_chunk: The ASCENT trial was a phase 3 randomized study evaluating sacituzumab govitecan (SG), a TROP-2-directed antibody-drug conjugate, versus physician's choice of single-agent chemotherapy in patients with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (mTNBC) who had received at least two prior therapies. A total of 468 patients without brain metastases were included in the primary efficacy population. Sacituzumab govitecan demonstrated a significant improvement in median PFS of 5.6 months versus 1.7 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.41, 95% CI 0.32-0.52, p<0.001) and median OS of 12.1 months versus 6.7 months (HR 0.48, 95% CI 0.38-0.59, p<0.001). The objective response rate was 35% versus 5%. Responses were observed regardless of TROP-2 expression level, although patients with high TROP-2 had numerically better outcomes. Grade 3 or higher adverse events with SG included neutropenia (51%), leukopenia (10%), diarrhea (10%), and anemia (8%). Febrile neutropenia occurred in 6% of SG patients. This trial established sacituzumab govitecan as a standard later-line therapy for mTNBC and validated TROP-2 as a therapeutic target in breast cancer.

title: Enfortumab Vedotin plus Pembrolizumab in Previously Untreated Advanced Urothelial Carcinoma (EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39)
text_chunk: EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 was a phase 3 randomized trial comparing enfortumab vedotin (EV) plus pembrolizumab with platinum-based chemotherapy (gemcitabine plus cisplatin or carboplatin) as first-line treatment for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma. A total of 886 patients were enrolled regardless of PD-L1 status or cisplatin eligibility. The EV plus pembrolizumab combination showed a dramatic improvement in PFS with a median of 12.5 months versus 6.3 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.45, 95% CI 0.38-0.54, p<0.001). Median OS was significantly longer at 31.5 months versus 16.1 months (HR 0.47, 95% CI 0.38-0.58, p<0.001). The confirmed ORR was 67.7% versus 44.4%, with complete response rates of 29.1% versus 12.5%. Benefits were consistent across cisplatin-eligible and cisplatin-ineligible patients, across PD-L1 subgroups, and regardless of liver metastasis status. Grade 3 or higher treatment-related adverse events occurred in 55.9% with EV plus pembrolizumab versus 69.5% with chemotherapy. Skin reactions, peripheral neuropathy, and hyperglycemia were notable with EV. This trial established a new first-line standard of care for advanced urothelial carcinoma.

title: Mirvetuximab Soravtansine in FRalpha-Positive Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer (MIRASOL)
text_chunk: MIRASOL (GOG 3045/ENGOT-ov55) was a phase 3 randomized trial comparing mirvetuximab soravtansine (MIRV), a folate receptor alpha (FRalpha)-directed antibody-drug conjugate, with investigator's choice chemotherapy in patients with FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant ovarian cancer who had received 1-3 prior lines of therapy. A total of 453 patients were enrolled following FRalpha testing by immunohistochemistry (PS2+ score of 75% or higher). MIRV demonstrated a significant improvement in PFS with a median of 5.62 months versus 3.98 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.65, 95% CI 0.52-0.81, p<0.001). Overall survival was also significantly improved with median OS of 16.46 months versus 12.75 months (HR 0.67, 95% CI 0.50-0.89, p=0.005). The ORR was 42.3% for MIRV versus 15.9% for chemotherapy. Grade 3 or higher adverse events with MIRV included blurred vision (9%), keratopathy (8%), and neutropenia (5%). This was the first phase 3 trial to demonstrate an OS benefit with a FRalpha-targeted ADC in ovarian cancer and validated FRalpha as a predictive biomarker requiring companion diagnostic testing.

title: Zolbetuximab plus mFOLFOX6 in CLDN18.2-Positive HER2-Negative Gastric or Gastroesophageal Junction Adenocarcinoma (SPOTLIGHT)
text_chunk: SPOTLIGHT was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating zolbetuximab, a monoclonal antibody targeting claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2), in combination with mFOLFOX6 chemotherapy versus placebo plus mFOLFOX6 as first-line treatment for CLDN18.2-positive, HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic gastric or gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) adenocarcinoma. Patients were required to have CLDN18.2 expression on at least 75% of tumor cells by immunohistochemistry. A total of 565 patients were randomized. Zolbetuximab plus mFOLFOX6 showed a significant improvement in median PFS of 10.61 months versus 8.67 months for placebo plus mFOLFOX6 (HR 0.751, 95% CI 0.59-0.94, p=0.0066). Median OS was 18.23 months versus 15.54 months (HR 0.750, 95% CI 0.60-0.94, p=0.0053). The most common adverse events with zolbetuximab were nausea (82.4%), vomiting (67.4%), and decreased appetite (44.4%), consistent with CLDN18.2-mediated gastric effects. This trial validated CLDN18.2 as a novel therapeutic target in gastric cancer and represents the first targeted therapy specifically for this biomarker-defined subset.

title: Tarlatamab for Patients with Previously Treated Small-Cell Lung Cancer (DeLLphi-301)
text_chunk: DeLLphi-301 was a phase 2 open-label trial evaluating tarlatamab, a delta-like ligand 3 (DLL3)-targeted bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE), in patients with relapsed or refractory small-cell lung cancer (SCLC) who had received at least two prior lines of therapy. A total of 220 patients were randomized to tarlatamab 10 mg or 100 mg every 2 weeks intravenously. At the primary analysis of the 10 mg cohort (99 patients), the objective response rate was 40% (95% CI 30-51), with 2% complete responses and 38% partial responses. The median duration of response was 9.7 months. Median PFS was 4.9 months and median OS was 14.3 months. Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) occurred in 53% of patients (1% grade 3), predominantly with the first dose. Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) occurred in 10% of patients. Common treatment-emergent adverse events included CRS, pyrexia, dysgeusia, decreased appetite, and constipation. Responses were observed regardless of DLL3 expression level, brain metastasis status, and prior therapy type. This trial demonstrated clinically meaningful activity of a bispecific T-cell engager in SCLC, a disease with limited treatment options beyond first-line chemo-immunotherapy.

title: Maintenance Olaparib in Patients with Newly Diagnosed Advanced Ovarian Cancer (SOLO-1)
text_chunk: SOLO-1 was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating olaparib as maintenance therapy versus placebo in patients with newly diagnosed advanced (FIGO stage III-IV) high-grade serous or endometrioid ovarian cancer harboring a BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation who had a complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. A total of 391 patients were enrolled. The results were transformative: median PFS was 56.0 months with olaparib versus 13.8 months with placebo (HR 0.33, 95% CI 0.25-0.43). At 5 years, 48.3% of olaparib patients remained progression-free versus 20.5% of placebo patients. The estimated 7-year OS rate was 67.0% for olaparib versus 46.5% for placebo (HR 0.55, 95% CI 0.40-0.76, p=0.0004). Grade 3 or higher adverse events with olaparib included anemia (22%), neutropenia (9%), and fatigue (4%). Myelodysplastic syndrome or acute myeloid leukemia occurred in 4.5% of olaparib patients. This trial established PARP inhibitor maintenance as a transformative strategy in BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer, producing the longest PFS ever reported in a phase 3 frontline ovarian cancer trial.

title: Alectinib versus Crizotinib in Untreated ALK-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (ALEX)
text_chunk: The ALEX trial was a phase 3 randomized, open-label study comparing alectinib, a second-generation ALK inhibitor, with crizotinib as first-line treatment for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. A total of 303 patients with ALK rearrangement confirmed by FISH or IHC were enrolled. Alectinib demonstrated a dramatically superior investigator-assessed median PFS of 34.8 months versus 10.9 months for crizotinib (HR 0.43, 95% CI 0.32-0.58). By independent review committee, median PFS was 25.7 months versus 10.4 months. CNS efficacy was a key differentiator: the CNS ORR was 81% with alectinib versus 50% with crizotinib in patients with measurable brain metastases, and alectinib reduced the incidence of CNS progression (12% vs 45%). The systemic ORR was 82.9% with alectinib versus 75.5% with crizotinib. Alectinib was better tolerated with grade 3-5 adverse events in 41% versus 50% for crizotinib. Common alectinib toxicities were myalgia (16%), anemia (20%), and elevated bilirubin (15%), while crizotinib caused more GI toxicity (nausea 48%, diarrhea 45%, vomiting 38%). This trial displaced crizotinib as first-line treatment for ALK-positive NSCLC and established alectinib as the NCCN-preferred first-line ALK inhibitor.

title: Combined BRAF and MEK Inhibition versus BRAF Inhibition Alone in Melanoma (COMBI-d)
text_chunk: COMBI-d was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating the combination of dabrafenib (BRAF inhibitor) plus trametinib (MEK inhibitor) versus dabrafenib plus placebo in patients with unresectable or metastatic BRAF V600E or V600K-mutant melanoma. The trial enrolled 423 previously untreated patients. The combination demonstrated a significant improvement in median PFS of 11.0 months versus 8.8 months for dabrafenib alone (HR 0.67, 95% CI 0.53-0.84, p=0.0004). The objective response rate was 69% versus 53%. Updated 3-year survival data showed an OS rate of 44% for the combination versus 32% for dabrafenib alone, with the longest follow-up suggesting a plateau in the survival curve around 20% at 5 years. A key mechanistic rationale was that MEK inhibitor co-treatment suppresses paradoxical MAPK pathway reactivation, reducing BRAF inhibitor-associated cutaneous toxicities. Indeed, the combination had lower rates of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (2% vs 9%), hyperkeratosis (7% vs 24%), and alopecia (3% vs 18%). Pyrexia was more common with the combination (51% vs 28%). This trial established dual MAPK pathway blockade as the standard targeted therapy approach for BRAF-mutant melanoma.

title: Palbociclib and Fulvestrant in Advanced Breast Cancer (PALOMA-3)
text_chunk: PALOMA-3 was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial comparing the CDK4/6 inhibitor palbociclib plus fulvestrant with placebo plus fulvestrant in women with hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer whose disease had progressed during prior endocrine therapy. A total of 521 patients were enrolled, including both pre/perimenopausal and postmenopausal women. Palbociclib plus fulvestrant significantly improved median PFS to 9.5 months versus 4.6 months for placebo plus fulvestrant (HR 0.46, 95% CI 0.36-0.59, p<0.001). Overall survival analysis showed a median OS of 34.9 months versus 28.0 months (HR 0.81, 95% CI 0.64-1.03, p=0.043 by one-sided test). Patients with sensitivity to prior endocrine therapy derived the greatest benefit. The most common grade 3-4 adverse event with palbociclib was neutropenia (65%), which was manageable with dose modifications and rarely associated with febrile neutropenia (2.5%). Other common side effects included fatigue, nausea, and anemia. Palbociclib did not impair quality of life. This trial, along with PALOMA-2, established CDK4/6 inhibitors as a backbone of HR-positive advanced breast cancer treatment.

title: Atezolizumab for First-Line Treatment of Metastatic Nonsquamous NSCLC (IMpower150)
text_chunk: IMpower150 was a phase 3 randomized trial evaluating atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) combined with bevacizumab, carboplatin, and paclitaxel (ABCP) versus bevacizumab, carboplatin, and paclitaxel (BCP) as first-line treatment for metastatic nonsquamous NSCLC. The trial enrolled 1202 patients, including those with EGFR mutations or ALK rearrangements who had progressed on targeted therapy. The ABCP regimen demonstrated a significant improvement in median PFS of 8.3 months versus 6.8 months for BCP (HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.52-0.74, p<0.001) and median OS of 19.2 months versus 14.7 months (HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.64-0.96, p=0.02). A unique feature of this trial was the inclusion of patients with liver metastases, EGFR/ALK aberrations, and low PD-L1 expression who are typically excluded from immunotherapy trials. The ABCP regimen showed benefit across PD-L1 subgroups and was the only chemo-IO regimen demonstrating benefit in EGFR/ALK-mutant patients. Grade 3-4 adverse events occurred in 55.7% with ABCP versus 47.7% with BCP. This trial established the ABCP regimen as an important first-line option, particularly for patients with liver metastases or those who have progressed on targeted therapies.

title: Encorafenib, Binimetinib, and Cetuximab in BRAF V600E-Mutated Colorectal Cancer (BEACON CRC)
text_chunk: The BEACON CRC trial was a phase 3 randomized study evaluating encorafenib (BRAF inhibitor) plus cetuximab (EGFR antibody), with or without binimetinib (MEK inhibitor), versus investigator's choice of cetuximab plus irinotecan or cetuximab plus FOLFIRI in patients with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer who had progressed after one or two prior lines of therapy. A total of 665 patients were enrolled. The triplet regimen (encorafenib + binimetinib + cetuximab) improved median OS to 9.0 months versus 5.4 months for control (HR 0.52, 95% CI 0.39-0.70, p<0.001). The doublet (encorafenib + cetuximab) showed a median OS of 8.4 months (HR 0.60, p<0.001). The ORR for the triplet was 26% versus 2% for control. The doublet was subsequently determined to have a favorable benefit-risk profile, as the addition of binimetinib provided only a modest incremental benefit with additional toxicity. Grade 3 or higher adverse events were 58% for the triplet, 50% for the doublet, and 61% for control. This trial addressed the unique biology of BRAF V600E CRC, which requires EGFR pathway co-inhibition to overcome feedback reactivation that limits BRAF monotherapy efficacy.

title: Alpelisib plus Fulvestrant in PIK3CA-Mutated HR-Positive Advanced Breast Cancer (SOLAR-1)
text_chunk: SOLAR-1 was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating alpelisib, an alpha-specific PI3K inhibitor, plus fulvestrant versus placebo plus fulvestrant in patients with PIK3CA-mutated, HR-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer that had progressed on or after prior endocrine therapy. A total of 572 patients were enrolled, with 341 in the PIK3CA-mutant cohort. In the PIK3CA-mutant cohort, alpelisib plus fulvestrant significantly improved median PFS to 11.0 months versus 5.7 months for placebo plus fulvestrant (HR 0.65, 95% CI 0.50-0.85, p<0.001). The ORR was 26.6% versus 12.8%. No PFS benefit was observed in the PIK3CA wild-type cohort. Grade 3-4 adverse events with alpelisib were notable and included hyperglycemia (36.6%), rash (9.9%), and diarrhea (6.7%). Hyperglycemia required active monitoring and management with metformin. Patients with pre-existing diabetes or fasting glucose above 140 mg/dL were excluded. Overall survival showed a numerical trend favoring alpelisib (39.3 vs 31.4 months, HR 0.86). This trial validated PIK3CA mutation as a predictive biomarker for PI3K inhibitor therapy and established alpelisib as a biomarker-driven treatment option after CDK4/6 inhibitor progression.

title: Venetoclax and Azacitidine in Previously Untreated Acute Myeloid Leukemia (VIALE-A)
text_chunk: VIALE-A was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating venetoclax, a BCL-2 inhibitor, combined with azacitidine versus placebo plus azacitidine in treatment-naive patients with AML who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy due to age (75 years or older) or comorbidities. A total of 431 patients were enrolled. The venetoclax combination demonstrated a significant OS improvement with a median OS of 14.7 months versus 9.6 months for azacitidine alone (HR 0.66, 95% CI 0.52-0.85, p<0.001). The composite complete remission rate (CR + CRi) was 66.4% versus 28.3%. Median time to first response was 1.3 months. The combination showed activity across molecular subgroups including IDH1/2 mutations (CR+CRi 75%), NPM1 mutations (CR+CRi 67%), and FLT3 mutations (CR+CRi 72%). Grade 3-4 adverse events included thrombocytopenia (45%), neutropenia (42%), febrile neutropenia (42%), and anemia (26%). Tumor lysis syndrome occurred in 1.4% of venetoclax patients. This trial established venetoclax plus azacitidine as the standard of care for older or unfit AML patients and represented a major advance in AML treatment.

title: Capivasertib plus Fulvestrant in Breast Cancer with AKT1, PIK3CA, or PTEN Alterations (CAPItello-291)
text_chunk: CAPItello-291 was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating capivasertib, a selective AKT inhibitor, plus fulvestrant versus placebo plus fulvestrant in patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer following progression on aromatase inhibitor therapy with or without a CDK4/6 inhibitor. A total of 708 patients were enrolled and stratified by AKT pathway alteration status (PIK3CA, AKT1, or PTEN alterations). In the overall population, capivasertib plus fulvestrant significantly improved median PFS to 7.2 months versus 3.6 months (HR 0.60, 95% CI 0.51-0.71, p<0.001). In the AKT pathway-altered subgroup (n=289), median PFS was 7.3 months versus 3.1 months (HR 0.50, 95% CI 0.38-0.65, p<0.001). The ORR in the altered subgroup was 28.8% versus 9.7%. Grade 3 or higher adverse events with capivasertib included diarrhea (9.3%), rash (12.1%), and hyperglycemia (2.3%). Capivasertib was administered on an intermittent schedule (4 days on, 3 days off) to manage toxicity. This trial validated AKT pathway alterations as predictive biomarkers and introduced a new treatment option for patients who have progressed on CDK4/6 inhibitor-based therapy.

title: Elacestrant versus Standard of Care for Estrogen Receptor-Positive, HER2-Negative Advanced Breast Cancer (EMERALD)
text_chunk: EMERALD was a phase 3 randomized, open-label trial evaluating elacestrant, an oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD), versus investigator's choice of standard endocrine monotherapy (fulvestrant or aromatase inhibitor) in patients with ER-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer who had received 1-2 prior lines of endocrine therapy including a CDK4/6 inhibitor. A total of 478 patients were enrolled, with prospective stratification by ESR1 mutation status via circulating tumor DNA. In the overall population, elacestrant improved median PFS to 3.8 months versus 1.9 months (HR 0.70, 95% CI 0.55-0.88, p=0.002). In the ESR1-mutant subgroup (n=228), the benefit was more pronounced with median PFS of 3.8 months versus 1.9 months (HR 0.55, 95% CI 0.39-0.77, p=0.0005). The 12-month PFS rate in ESR1-mutant patients was 26.8% versus 8.2%. Grade 3 or higher adverse events with elacestrant were nausea (2.6%), increased ALT (2.6%), and back pain (2.6%). This trial established ESR1 mutations as a predictive biomarker for oral SERD therapy and validated liquid biopsy-guided treatment selection in breast oncology.

title: Repotrectinib in ROS1 Fusion-Positive Advanced Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (TRIDENT-1)
text_chunk: TRIDENT-1 was a phase 1/2 registrational study evaluating repotrectinib, a next-generation ROS1 and TRK inhibitor designed to overcome resistance mutations including the solvent-front G2032R mutation, in patients with ROS1 fusion-positive advanced NSCLC. In 71 ROS1 TKI-naive patients, repotrectinib achieved an ORR of 79% (95% CI 68-88) with a median duration of response of 34.1 months. In 56 patients who had received one prior ROS1 TKI (but not chemotherapy), the ORR was 38% (95% CI 25-52). Among patients with prior crizotinib, the ORR was 42%. Notably, repotrectinib showed activity in patients with the G2032R solvent-front mutation, which confers resistance to first- and second-generation ROS1 inhibitors. The intracranial response rate in evaluable patients with measurable CNS disease was 83% in TKI-naive and 42% in previously treated cohorts. Grade 3-4 adverse events included dizziness (5%), hypoxia (3%), and dyspnea (3%). CNS effects included dizziness (58%) and dysgeusia (47%). This trial led to FDA approval of repotrectinib for ROS1-positive NSCLC and demonstrated clinical activity against solvent-front resistance mutations.

title: Dostarlimab for Deficient Mismatch Repair Recurrent or Advanced Endometrial Cancer (GARNET)
text_chunk: GARNET was a phase 1, single-arm, multicohort trial evaluating dostarlimab, an anti-PD-1 antibody, in patients with recurrent or advanced solid tumors including a dedicated cohort for dMMR/MSI-H endometrial cancer. Among 103 patients with dMMR/MSI-H endometrial cancer who had received at least one prior platinum-containing regimen, dostarlimab demonstrated an ORR of 44.7% (95% CI 34.9-54.8) by independent central review, with 12.6% complete responses and 32.0% partial responses. The median duration of response was not reached at the time of analysis, with 79.5% of responders maintaining response at 12 months. Median PFS was 5.5 months and median OS was not reached. Grade 3 or higher treatment-related adverse events occurred in 12.6% of patients, with fatigue (2.9%), anemia (1.9%), and diarrhea (1.9%) being most common. Immune-mediated adverse events of interest included hypothyroidism (8.7%), hyperthyroidism (2.9%), colitis (2.9%), and pneumonitis (1.9%). Responses were observed regardless of PD-L1 expression status, consistent with the high immunogenicity of dMMR/MSI-H tumors. This study contributed to the tumor-agnostic FDA approval of dostarlimab for dMMR solid tumors.

title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Previously Treated HER2-Low Advanced Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast04)
text_chunk: DESTINY-Breast04 was a landmark phase 3 randomized trial evaluating trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) versus physician's choice of chemotherapy in patients with HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-negative) metastatic breast cancer who had received one or two prior lines of chemotherapy. This trial was groundbreaking because it created a new treatment category for HER2-low breast cancer, previously grouped with HER2-negative disease. A total of 557 patients were enrolled, with 494 having HR-positive disease. In the HR-positive cohort, T-DXd improved median PFS to 10.1 months versus 5.4 months (HR 0.51, 95% CI 0.40-0.64, p<0.001) and median OS to 23.9 months versus 17.5 months (HR 0.64, 95% CI 0.48-0.86, p=0.003). In the overall population, median PFS was 9.9 versus 5.1 months (HR 0.50) and median OS was 23.4 versus 16.8 months (HR 0.64). The ORR was 52.3% versus 16.3%. ILD occurred in 12.1% of T-DXd patients (0.8% grade 5). This trial redefined breast cancer classification by demonstrating that HER2-low tumors are therapeutically targetable with T-DXd, expanding the eligible population by approximately 50% of all breast cancers.

title: Osimertinib in Resected EGFR-Mutated Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (ADAURA)
text_chunk: ADAURA was a phase 3 randomized, double-blind trial evaluating adjuvant osimertinib versus placebo in patients with completely resected stage IB-IIIA EGFR-mutated (exon 19 deletion or L858R) NSCLC following standard adjuvant chemotherapy when indicated. A total of 682 patients were enrolled. In the stage II-IIIA population (primary endpoint), osimertinib demonstrated a dramatic improvement in disease-free survival with a 2-year DFS rate of 90% versus 44% for placebo (HR 0.17, 95% CI 0.12-0.23, p<0.001). In the overall population (stages IB-IIIA), median DFS was 65.8 months for osimertinib versus 28.1 months for placebo (HR 0.27). CNS DFS was markedly improved, with a CNS recurrence rate of 2% versus 11% at 24 months. The mature OS analysis demonstrated a statistically significant improvement with 5-year OS rates of 88% versus 78% in the stage II-IIIA population (HR 0.49, 95% CI 0.33-0.73, p<0.001). Grade 3 or higher adverse events occurred in 23% with osimertinib versus 14% with placebo. This trial established adjuvant osimertinib as the standard of care for resected EGFR-mutant NSCLC and represented the first adjuvant targeted therapy to demonstrate an OS benefit in lung cancer.

title: Capmatinib in MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutated Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (GEOMETRY mono-1)
text_chunk: GEOMETRY mono-1 was a phase 2 multi-cohort study evaluating capmatinib, a highly selective MET inhibitor, in patients with MET exon 14 skipping mutation-positive advanced NSCLC. The study included treatment-naive and previously treated cohorts. In 28 treatment-naive patients, capmatinib achieved an ORR of 67.9% (95% CI 47.6-84.1) with a median DOR of 12.6 months. In 69 previously treated patients (1-2 prior lines), the ORR was 40.6% (95% CI 28.9-53.1) with a median DOR of 9.7 months. The overall disease control rate was 78.3% in previously treated and 96.4% in treatment-naive patients. Intracranial activity was observed, with a brain response rate of 54% among evaluable patients with brain metastases. Grade 3-4 adverse events included peripheral edema (8%), nausea (4%), fatigue (5%), and increased creatinine (3%). MET exon 14 skipping mutations occur in approximately 3-4% of NSCLC, predominantly in elderly patients and in sarcomatoid histology. This trial led to accelerated FDA approval of capmatinib as the first MET inhibitor specifically approved for MET exon 14 skipping mutated NSCLC and underscored the importance of comprehensive genomic profiling.

title: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab in Microsatellite-Instability-High Metastatic Colorectal Cancer (CheckMate-8HW)
text_chunk: CheckMate-8HW was a phase 3 randomized, open-label trial evaluating nivolumab plus ipilimumab (nivo+ipi) versus chemotherapy (with or without targeted therapy) as first-line treatment for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic colorectal cancer. The nivolumab plus ipilimumab arm used nivolumab 240 mg every 2 weeks plus ipilimumab 1 mg/kg every 6 weeks, an optimized low-dose ipilimumab regimen to improve tolerability. At the prespecified interim analysis, nivo+ipi demonstrated a significant PFS improvement over chemotherapy with a median PFS not yet reached versus 5.9 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.21, 95% CI 0.14-0.32, p<0.0001). The 12-month PFS rate was 79% for nivo+ipi versus 21% for chemotherapy. The confirmed ORR was 69% versus 38%, with a complete response rate of 13% versus 4%. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 23% with nivo+ipi versus 48% with chemotherapy. The trial also included a nivolumab monotherapy arm, with a comparison of nivo+ipi versus nivolumab alone as a co-primary endpoint. This trial reinforced dual checkpoint inhibition as the optimal immunotherapy approach for MSI-H CRC and provided the most robust data for immunotherapy in first-line MSI-H colorectal cancer.
